title: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom
rawPassageText:
READING PASSAGE 3
You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27-40, which are based on Reading Passage 3 below.

The Productive Unease of Boredom

A passenger waiting for a delayed train lifts his phone, lowers it, looks along the platform, and lifts it again. Nothing urgent has arrived; in fact, nothing has arrived at all. Yet the small interval of emptiness feels almost like a problem requiring repair. In cafés, school corridors and office lifts, similar scenes are repeated countless times each day. Boredom, once an ordinary companion of waiting, has increasingly come to be treated as a technical failure: a gap that should be filled as quickly as possible.

It would be tempting to conclude that modern devices have created boredom, but this would mistake a new response for a new condition. People have always complained of dull lessons, slow journeys and repetitive work. What has changed is the speed with which many people can now escape such moments. A person who feels the first signs of restlessness can summon entertainment, news, conversation or shopping in seconds. This does not necessarily make life more interesting. It may simply make boredom harder to recognise before it has been covered over.

Psychologists who study boredom often distinguish it from laziness or simple tiredness. A tired person may want rest; an apathetic person may not care what happens next. A bored person, by contrast, usually wants to be engaged but cannot find anything that feels worth engaging with. Dr Mara Ellison, who studies attention at Lydgate University, describes boredom as a form of "attentional weather": not a permanent flaw in character, but a passing mental condition in which the mind is unsettled and looking for direction. This definition helps explain why boredom can feel both empty and active. The bored mind is not switched off; it is searching without settling.

Schools and workplaces have generally tried to reduce this state, and for understandable reasons. Long periods of boredom can produce frustration, poor performance and disengagement. A factory worker repeating the same narrow action for hours, or a student who sees no purpose in a lesson, is unlikely to be helped by being told that boredom is good for the soul. Nevertheless, treating boredom only as an enemy may be too simple. Like hunger, discomfort or confusion, boredom may sometimes be information. It can signal that a task is too easy, too fragmented, too controlled, or insufficiently connected to a person's own questions.

This possibility was explored in a study led by Armand Calder at Northbridge College. Ninety volunteers were randomly assigned to one of three opening tasks. One group copied columns of telephone numbers from a directory. A second watched a neutral documentary about building regulations. A third solved short word puzzles that were mildly demanding but not especially enjoyable. No participant was told that the opening task was connected to the next stage of the experiment. Afterwards, all participants were asked to list as many possible uses as they could for a cardboard tube. The telephone-number group, who reported the highest level of boredom, produced fewer obvious suggestions at first, but eventually generated the widest range of categories. Their answers moved from storage and packaging to musical instruments, children's games and imaginary scientific devices. Calder did not claim that boredom automatically creates imagination. His more careful conclusion was that an undemanding task may leave the mind free to wander, and that such wandering can sometimes help people move beyond standard responses.

A related pattern appears in some workplaces, though only under certain conditions. In interviews with office employees, management researcher Helen Voss found that dull intervals sometimes led workers to improve small routines: they redesigned forms, reorganised shared files, or found quicker ways to answer common customer questions. However, this happened mainly when employees had enough freedom to alter their work. Where every action was tightly monitored, boredom was more likely to produce resentment than invention. Voss therefore argues that boredom is not useful by itself. Its value depends on whether people are allowed to respond to the signal it provides.

Some educators have begun experimenting with this idea on a smaller scale. They do not aim to make lessons tedious; rather, they create brief pauses in which students must stay with an unresolved problem before receiving an explanation. In one history class, for example, students were shown a photograph of a crowded city street from the early twentieth century and asked to observe it silently for three minutes. Several students were restless at first. Yet when discussion began, they noticed details that would probably have been missed if the teacher had immediately explained the image: the absence of cars, the direction of people's gaze, the clothing of children, and the advertisements above the shops. The pause did not lower the standard of the lesson. It changed the students' role from receivers of information to investigators of it.

Museums have reported similar results with "slow-looking" sessions, where visitors spend extended time with a single object before reading the label beside it. The method can initially feel uncomfortable, especially to visitors used to moving quickly from display to display. But the discomfort is part of the exercise. Instead of consuming a prepared explanation, viewers begin to form questions of their own. They may still need expert knowledge, just as students still need teachers, but the knowledge arrives after curiosity has had time to develop.

None of this means that boredom should be romanticised. Chronic boredom can be a sign of badly designed work, uninspired teaching or a lack of meaningful choice. But the opposite extreme is also worth questioning. A life from which every empty moment has been removed may not be a richer life, only a more crowded one. If boredom is a signal, then the aim should not be to silence it instantly, but to learn when it is pointing toward something that needs to change.

Questions 27-31
Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.
Write the correct letter in boxes 27-31 on your answer sheet.

27 The writer describes the passenger waiting for a delayed train in order to show
A how public transport has become less reliable.
B how people often try to remove moments of emptiness.
C how mobile phones have made people more sociable.
D how waiting can improve a person's concentration.

28 In the second paragraph, what point does the writer make about modern devices?
A They have made boredom more common than it used to be.
B They are most useful when people have urgent tasks to complete.
C They have changed how quickly people can respond to boredom.
D They prevent people from feeling interested in their surroundings.

29 What does Dr Mara Ellison's phrase "attentional weather" suggest about boredom?
A It is a temporary mental state rather than a fixed personal weakness.
B It is caused mainly by external physical conditions.
C It is more dangerous than most people realise.
D It is impossible for psychologists to measure accurately.

30 What did Armand Calder's study suggest?
A Bored participants completed the later task more quickly than others.
B A neutral documentary was more effective than a demanding puzzle.
C An undemanding activity may help people produce less predictable ideas.
D People are more creative when they are told to expect a creativity test.

31 What is the main point made about boredom in workplaces?
A It is useful only when workers are prevented from changing their routines.
B It can lead to improvements if workers have some control over their tasks.
C It usually has the same effect on all types of employees.
D It is best reduced by giving workers more detailed instructions.

Questions 32-36
Complete the summary using the list of words and phrases, A-I, below.
Write the correct letter, A-I, in boxes 32-36 on your answer sheet.

Boredom in learning environments

Some teachers have introduced short periods of 32 ____________ before giving students an explanation. These pauses are not intended to make lessons 33 ____________. Instead, they allow students to spend time with 34 ____________ and develop their own observations. In the history lesson described, students who were initially 35 ____________ later noticed several important details in a photograph. According to the writer, this approach changes students from passive receivers of information into 36 ____________.

A tedious
B silence
C uncertainty
D investigators
E restless
F entertainment
G standards
H expert knowledge
I instructions

Questions 37-40
Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in Reading Passage 3?
In boxes 37-40 on your answer sheet, write

YES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
NO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
NOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this

37 Modern devices created boredom.
38 In Calder's study, participants knew that the first task was connected to the later creativity task.
39 Boredom is more common among teenagers than among adults.
40 Immediately removing every moment of boredom may prevent people from noticing a useful signal.
rawAnswerKey:
Question 27: The writer describes the passenger waiting for a delayed train in order to show

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: Yet the small interval of emptiness feels almost like a problem requiring repair. In cafés, school corridors and office lifts, similar scenes are repeated countless times each day.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะ opening scene แสดงคนยกโทรศัพท์ซ้ำ ๆ เพราะช่วง emptiness รู้สึกเหมือนปัญหาที่ต้องแก้ ซึ่ง paraphrase เป็น people try to remove moments of emptiness ตัวเลือก A/C/D ไม่ตรงจุดประสงค์ของตัวอย่าง passenger (MC เรียงตามลำดับย่อหน้าเปิดเรื่อง)

Paraphrased Vocabulary: remove moments of emptiness = emptiness feels like a problem requiring repair; passenger scene = lifts his phone, lowers it, looks along the platform

Question 28: In the second paragraph, what point does the writer make about modern devices?

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: What has changed is the speed with which many people can now escape such moments. A person who feels the first signs of restlessness can summon entertainment, news, conversation or shopping in seconds.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C เพราะย่อหน้าสองชี้ว่า devices ไม่ได้สร้าง boredom ใหม่ แต่เปลี่ยน speed ที่คนหนี boredom ได้ ตัวเลือก A ผิดเพราะ passage ปฏิเสธว่า devices created boredom ตัวเลือก B/D ไม่ใช่ประเด็นหลักของย่อหน้า

Paraphrased Vocabulary: respond to boredom = escape such moments; how quickly = speed with which; modern devices = summon entertainment, news, conversation or shopping in seconds

Question 29: What does Dr Mara Ellison's phrase "attentional weather" suggest about boredom?

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: describes boredom as a form of "attentional weather": not a permanent flaw in character, but a passing mental condition in which the mind is unsettled and looking for direction.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A เพราะ Ellison เปรียบ boredom เป็น passing mental condition ไม่ใช่ permanent flaw in character ซึ่งตรง temporary mental state rather than fixed personal weakness ตัวเลือก B/C/D ไม่มีหลักฐานในประโยคนิยามนี้

Paraphrased Vocabulary: attentional weather = passing mental condition; temporary = not permanent; fixed personal weakness = permanent flaw in character

Question 30: What did Armand Calder's study suggest?

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: Calder did not claim that boredom automatically creates imagination. His more careful conclusion was that an undemanding task may leave the mind free to wander, and that such wandering can sometimes help people move beyond standard responses.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C เพราะ Calder สรุปอย่างระมัดระวังว่า undemanding task อาจให้ mind wander และช่วย move beyond standard responses ไม่ใช่ boredom สร้าง imagination อัตโนมัติ ตัวเลือก A/D ขัดกับข้อมูลการทดลอง

Paraphrased Vocabulary: undemanding activity = undemanding task; less predictable ideas = move beyond standard responses / widest range of categories

Question 31: What is the main point made about boredom in workplaces?

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: dull intervals sometimes led workers to improve small routines... However, this happened mainly when employees had enough freedom to alter their work. Where every action was tightly monitored, boredom was more likely to produce resentment than invention.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะ Helen Voss พบว่า boredom อาจนำไปสู่การปรับปรุงงานเมื่อ workers มี freedom to alter their work แต่ถ้าถูก monitor แน่นอนจะเกิด resentment ตัวเลือก A กลับความหมาย ตัวเลือก C/D ไม่ตรงข้อมูลสัมภาษณ์

Paraphrased Vocabulary: improvements = improve small routines; control over tasks = freedom to alter their work; workplaces = office employees

Question 32: Some teachers have introduced short periods of .................... before giving students an explanation.

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: they create brief pauses in which students must stay with an unresolved problem before receiving an explanation. In one history class, for example, students were shown a photograph... and asked to observe it silently for three minutes.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B (silence) เพราะ summary ถาม short periods of ___ ก่อน explanation ซึ่งตรง observe it silently / brief pauses ใน passage ไม่ใช่ entertainment หรือ instructions

Paraphrased Vocabulary: short periods of ___ = brief pauses / observe it silently; before explanation = before receiving an explanation; word bank = B silence

Question 33: These pauses are not intended to make lessons .................... .

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: They do not aim to make lessons tedious; rather, they create brief pauses in which students must stay with an unresolved problem before receiving an explanation.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A (tedious) เพราะ passage ระบุชัดว่า do not aim to make lessons tedious ตรงกับ not intended to make lessons ___ ใน summary

Paraphrased Vocabulary: not intended = do not aim; make lessons ___ = make lessons tedious; word bank = A tedious

Question 34: they allow students to spend time with .................... and develop their own observations.

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: students must stay with an unresolved problem before receiving an explanation.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C (uncertainty) เพราะ students ต้อง stay with an unresolved problem ก่อนได้คำอธิบาย ซึ่ง paraphrase เป็น spend time with uncertainty ไม่ใช่ expert knowledge ที่มาทีหลัง

Paraphrased Vocabulary: spend time with ___ = stay with an unresolved problem; develop observations = noticed details / investigators of it; word bank = C uncertainty

Question 35: students who were initially .................... later noticed several important details in a photograph.

Correct Answer: E

Accepted Answers: E

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: Several students were restless at first. Yet when discussion began, they noticed details that would probably have been missed if the teacher had immediately explained the image

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ E (restless) เพราะ passage บอก Several students were restless at first ตรงกับ initially ___ ใน summary

Paraphrased Vocabulary: initially ___ = restless at first; noticed details = noticed details in a photograph; word bank = E restless

Question 36: changes students from passive receivers of information into .................... .

Correct Answer: D

Accepted Answers: D

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: It changed the students' role from receivers of information to investigators of it.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ D (investigators) เพราะ passage ใช้คำว่า investigators of it ตรงกับ passive receivers into ___ ใน summary

Paraphrased Vocabulary: passive receivers = receivers of information; into ___ = investigators of it; word bank = D investigators

Question 37:Modern devices created boredom.

Correct Answer: NO

Accepted Answers: NO

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: It would be tempting to conclude that modern devices have created boredom, but this would mistake a new response for a new condition.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ NO เพราะ writer บอกว่า tempting to conclude devices created boredom แต่ this would mistake a new response for a new condition โจทย์ใช้ The writer believes devices created boredom ซึ่งขัดกับข้อความที่ writer ปฏิเสธแนวคิดนี้ (YNNG เรียงตามลำดับเนื้อหาในบทความ)

Paraphrased Vocabulary: writer believes devices created boredom = tempting to conclude... but mistake; created boredom = new response for a new condition

Question 38: In Calder's study, participants knew that the first task was connected to the later creativity task.

Correct Answer: NO

Accepted Answers: NO

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: No participant was told that the opening task was connected to the next stage of the experiment.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ NO เพราะ passage ระบุชัดว่า No participant was told that the opening task was connected to the next stage โจทย์บอก participants knew ซึ่งขัดกับข้อมูลโดยตรง

Paraphrased Vocabulary: participants knew = was told; first task connected = opening task was connected to the next stage; creativity task = list uses for a cardboard tube

Question 39: Boredom is more common among teenagers than among adults.

Correct Answer: NOT GIVEN

Accepted Answers: NOT GIVEN

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: In cafés, school corridors and office lifts, similar scenes are repeated countless times each day.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ NOT GIVEN เพราะ passage พูดถึง students, workers และ people ทั่วไป แต่ไม่เปรียบเทียบว่า boredom เกิดบ่อยกว่าใน teenagers หรือ adults คำว่า students ปรากฏแต่ไม่ตอบ comparison ในโจทย์

Paraphrased Vocabulary: teenagers vs adults = not compared; more common = no frequency comparison; NOT GIVEN = keywords in passage but claim untestable

Question 40:Immediately removing every moment of boredom may prevent people from noticing a useful signal.

Correct Answer: YES

Accepted Answers: YES

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Feb 2026 Passage 3 - The Productive Unease of Boredom

Exact Portion: A life from which every empty moment has been removed may not be a richer life, only a more crowded one. If boredom is a signal, then the aim should not be to silence it instantly, but to learn when it is pointing toward something that needs to change.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ YES เพราะ writer บอกว่าการลบ empty moments ทุกครั้งอาจทำให้ชีวิต crowded ไม่ใช่ richer และ boredom เป็น signal ที่ไม่ควร silence instantly ซึ่งสอดคล้องกับโจทย์ว่าการ remove boredom ทันทีอาจทำให้พลาด useful signal

Paraphrased Vocabulary: removing every moment of boredom = every empty moment has been removed; useful signal = boredom is a signal; silence it instantly = should not be to silence it instantly
